title: Prinsesstårta
subtitle: Swedish princess cake with marzipan dome and whipped cream
excerpt: Prinsesstårta is Sweden's celebration cake: three layers of genoise sponge with vanilla custard, jam, and a thick dome of whipped cream, all wrapped in a sheet of pale green marzipan and finished with a pink marzipan rose. It is a show-piece cake that requires patience and confidence with cream, but each element is straightforward. The custard can be made a day ahead; assembly takes about an hour.
sourceNotes: Prinsesstårta was created by Jenny Åkerström, tutor to the daughters of Prince Carl of Sweden, in the early twentieth century; her cookbook 'Prinsessornas Kokbok' (1929) contains the original recipe. The cake became Sweden's national celebration cake and is listed in the Swedish Cultural Heritage Foundation. The pale green marzipan and pink rose have been consistent features since the 1930s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The vanilla custard can be made 24 hours ahead and refrigerated. The sponge can be baked and cooled the day before. Assemble and marzipan on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A whole-egg foam cake where eggs and sugar are whisked together over gentle heat until pale and tripled in volume before flour and melted butter are folded in. The technique produces a fine, slightly dry crumb that holds its shape when sliced and layered. Unlike a Victoria sponge, which relies on butter for its lift, the genoise relies entirely on the egg foam — so vigorous whisking and careful folding are both essential.

content.text: The dome of cream is the defining element. It is shaped by mounding the whipped cream in the centre of the top layer before draping the marzipan; as the marzipan settles, it takes the dome shape. The marzipan must be rolled thin (about 2.5 mm) or it will crack when you bring it over the sides. Work quickly; marzipan dries and becomes brittle as it sits.

items.prepNote: pale green (knead in a small drop of green food colouring)

content.text: Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20 cm round tin. Set a large heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water. Add 4 eggs and 150 g sugar and whisk over the heat for 5 to 7 minutes until the mixture is pale, tripled in volume, and holds a ribbon for 5 seconds. This is a

. Remove from the heat. Sift in 120 g flour in three additions, folding gently after each. Fold in 40 g melted butter. Pour into the tin and bake for 22 to 25 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool completely on a rack, then slice into 3 horizontal layers.

Heat 300 ml milk until just below simmering. Whisk 3 egg yolks, 70 g sugar, 25 g cornflour, and 1 tsp vanilla paste. Pour the hot milk over the yolk mixture in a thin stream, whisking. Return to a medium heat and stir continuously until thick and bubbling; about 3 minutes. Press cling film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold.

Place the bottom sponge layer on a serving plate. Spread with 3 tbsp raspberry jam. Spread half the cold custard over the jam. Add the second sponge layer. Spread with the remaining custard. Whip 400 ml cold double cream with 2 tbsp icing sugar to firm peaks. Spread a thin layer of cream over the second sponge layer, then mound the remaining cream in a dome in the centre of the top layer, using a spatula to smooth it. Place the third sponge layer on top and press very gently. The cream dome should show slightly through the top layer.

Dust a clean surface with icing sugar. Roll 500 g pale green marzipan into a circle about 35 cm across and 2.5 mm thick. Lift on the rolling pin and drape over the dome. Smooth from the top down, easing the marzipan onto the sides without tearing. Trim the excess level with the base. Chill for at least 2 hours before serving. Add a small marzipan rose on top if you wish.

items.problem: Marzipan cracked when draped over the dome
items.solution: The marzipan was too thick, too cold, or left too long before covering. Roll to 2.5 mm and work quickly. If it tears, press the tear together from the underside — once chilled it will hold.

items.problem: Genoise sponge was dense and heavy
items.solution: The egg foam was not whisked enough, or the flour was folded in too vigorously. The ribbon test is the key indicator: the mixture should hold a thick ribbon for at least 5 seconds after the whisk is lifted. Fold the flour in the minimum number of strokes needed to eliminate streaks.

items.problem: Cream dome collapsed when covered with marzipan
items.solution: The cream was not whipped to firm peaks, or the marzipan was too heavy. Whip the cream until it holds a stiff peak that does not droop when the bowl is turned. Chill the assembled cake for 30 minutes before covering if the kitchen is warm.
